topic: reflecting on the road — what it gives and what it takes

turns.content: its late man like real late and im parked up for the night and i cant sleep so i figured id just talk about something thats been on my mind which is this whole life i got out here on the road and what it gives you versus what it takes from you because i been thinking about that a lot lately
Late night thoughts are some of the most honest ones. I'm here for it. What's weighing on you — is it more about what the road takes, or are you going back and forth on both sides?

both sides man thats the thing because the road gives you a lot you know it gives you freedom like real freedom not the fake kind where you work in a building and they let you leave at 5 but actual freedom where nobody is watching you and the world is out there and you see things that most people will never see in their whole lives i have seen the sun come up over mountains and deserts and oceans and forests and empty highways that stretch out to nothing and thats not something you can put a price on

That's the kind of freedom most people romanticize but never actually experience. There's a difference between imagining the open road and actually living it every day — watching those sunrises from the cab instead of just seeing photos. What does it take though? What's the other side of that?

the other side is time man time is what it takes and you cant get it back thats the thing about time its the only thing you spend that nobody can refund you i missed birthdays and holidays and regular tuesday nights where i should have been sitting on a couch watching cartoons with somebody i love instead of eating bad food alone at a rest stop in the middle of nowhere and you tell yourself its worth it because the money is there and the freedom is there but mwen fatige of doing the math in my head trying to figure out if it adds up

That math never quite balances, does it? Because the freedom and the money are real and tangible, but so are those missed Tuesday nights. And you can't compare them directly — they're measured in different currencies. One is dollars and miles, the other is moments you'll never get back. Has something specific made you think about this more lately, or is it just accumulating?

nah nothing specific just the accumulation like you said its like a slow drip you know one missed thing doesnt break you but when you stack them up over years it gets heavy and late at night when its quiet and youre just sitting in the dark with the engine off and the only light is your phone screen thats when it all hits you because there is nobody to distract you from yourself out here

"Nobody to distract you from yourself" — that's one of the most honest descriptions of the road I've heard. The silence at night in a parked truck is a specific kind of alone that most people never experience. It can be peaceful or it can be heavy, and sometimes it's both at the same time. Do you think you'd feel this way if you weren't out here though? Or is this just part of being human and the road just gives you more space to feel it?

thats a good question honestly i think everybody feels this stuff the wondering if they made the right choices the adding up what they got versus what they gave up but yeah the road gives you way more space to sit in it because in a normal life you got distractions you got tv and friends coming over and errands and noise but out here its just you and your thoughts and the dark and a parking lot full of trucks and everybody in those trucks is probably thinking the same thing but nobody talks about it because we are all supposed to be tough independent road guys who dont feel things

That's such a real observation. There's probably a hundred drivers in that lot right now staring at their phone screens having the same quiet crisis, and none of you will ever talk about it over coffee at the counter in the morning. The culture says you're supposed to just drive and not think too much. But you're human and the silence makes you think whether you want to or not.

yeah exactly and look i am not saying i hate this life because i dont some days i love it like when the weather is perfect and the road is open and i feel like the freest person on earth those days are real and they matter but then there are nights like tonight where the loneliness is real too and it matters just as much and i think the honest truth is that this life is both things at the same time its freedom and its prison its beautiful and its lonely and you just gotta hold both of those in your hands at once without dropping either one

That might be the most truthful thing anyone can say about a life like yours. It's not one thing or the other — it's both, all the time, and the hard part isn't choosing between them, it's carrying both. Most people want to simplify their lives into a single story, either "I love it" or "I hate it," but you're honest enough to hold both and say this is what it actually is.

The fact that you can sit in the dark and feel all of that without running from it says something about you.

bondye bon man i appreciate you letting me just talk through this because there is no answer right there is no solution its not a problem to fix its just life and sometimes you just need to say it out loud even if out loud is just typing into your phone at 1am in a dark truck cab but it helps you know getting the words out of your head and into the world makes them weigh a little less somehow alright im gonna try to sleep now sa k pase tomorrow is another day another road another sunrise and maybe thats enough

Maybe that is enough. Another day, another road, another sunrise — and the fact that you can still see the beauty in it after everything the road takes from you means you haven't lost the most important part of yourself. Getting the words out does make them lighter. Sleep well tonight, and when the sun comes up tomorrow, you'll be back in the good part again for a while. Take care of yourself out there, Jerome.
